title: Project Cheetah and Associated Habitats
focus: Cheetah reintroduction details, comparison of Asiatic and African cheetahs, and Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary profile.
rationale: Covers a major flagship species reintroduction program, critical IUCN/CITES statuses of two cheetah subspecies, and geographical/ecological details of Gandhi Sagar WLS.
sectionExcerpt: Project Cheetah, launched in 2022 under the umbrella of Project Tiger and Elephant, aims to reintroduce cheetahs to India. Implemented by NTCA with WII's technical assistance, it currently supports cheetahs in Kuno National Park. The Asiatic Cheetah is Critically Endangered (IUCN) and found only in Iran, while the African Cheetah is Vulnerable. Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary in Madhya Pradesh (Khathiar-Gir dry deciduous forest, River Chambal) is also highlighted, featuring flora like Salai and fauna like Dholes and Mugger crocodiles, alongside Chaturbhuj Nala rock shelters.
evidenceBullets: Project Cheetah operates under Project Tiger and Elephant; implemented by NTCA with WII technical assistance.
Asiatic Cheetah: IUCN Critically Endangered, CITES Appendix I, WPA Schedule I; found only in Iran; extinct in India since 1952.
African Cheetah: IUCN Vulnerable, CITES Appendix I; Namibia holds the largest population.
Cheetahs are the world's fastest mammals and do not roar unlike other big cats.
Gandhi Sagar WLS: Located in MP (Nimar region), features Khathiar-Gir dry deciduous forest, River Chambal, and Chaturbhuj Nala rock shelters.

title: Tiger Reserves and Supreme Court Directives
focus: Supreme Court guidelines on tiger reserve management, core vs. buffer zone regulations, and statutory provisions for Tiger Reserves under WPA 1972.
rationale: Highly examinable polity and environment intersection detailing SC bans on core area safaris, NTCA's statutory role, and Human-Wildlife Conflict (HWC) compensation rules.
sectionExcerpt: The Supreme Court issued directions to protect tiger reserves, banning tiger safaris and night tourism in core/critical habitats. Safaris are permitted only on non-forest or degraded forest land in buffer zones. Commercial mining and major hydro projects are prohibited in buffer areas. Human-Wildlife Conflicts (HWCs) are to be treated as Natural Disasters with a uniform 10 lakh ex-gratia for human deaths. Tiger Reserves are declared under WPA 1972 by State Governments on NTCA's advice, and boundary alterations require NTCA recommendation and National Board for Wild Life (NBWL) approval.
evidenceBullets: SC banned tiger safaris and night tourism in core areas/designated tiger corridors.
Safaris are permitted only on non-forest or degraded forest land in buffer zones.
Human-Wildlife Conflicts (HWCs) to be granted Natural Disaster status with 10 lakh ex-gratia for human deaths.
Tiger Reserves consist of inviolable Core Areas and regulated Buffer Areas (sustainable use zones).
Reserves are notified by State Governments under WPA 1972 on the advice of NTCA.
Boundary alterations of Tiger Reserves require NTCA recommendation and NBWL approval.

title: International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA)
focus: India's role as the host for the IBCA Headquarters.
rationale: A brief but significant international environmental initiative hosted by India.
sectionExcerpt: India and the International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA) signed a Headquarters Agreement to host the IBCA Headquarters and Secretariat in India, facilitating the efficient discharge of its official functions.
evidenceBullets: India signed an agreement to host the IBCA Headquarters and Secretariat.
